mission.title: Install and Configure Cluster API (CAPI)
mission.description: Cluster API (CAPI) is a Kubernetes operator that provides declarative APIs and tooling to simplify the provisioning, upgrading, and management of multiple Kubernetes clusters. It is particularly useful for platform operators who need to automate cluster lifecycle management across various infrastructure environments. Key trade-offs include the complexity of setup and the need for familiarity with Kubernetes concepts.

steps.title: Add the Cluster API Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official Cluster API Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration.

```bash

helm repo add cluster-api https://charts.cluster-api.sigs.k8s.io

```

> This repository contains the necessary charts for installation.

steps.title: Install the Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs)
steps.description:
CRDs are essential for the Cluster API to function correctly, as they define the custom resources used by the operator.

```bash

kubectl apply -f https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/cluster-api/releases/download/v1.12.5/cluster-api.crds.yaml

```

> This command installs the CRDs required for Cluster API.

steps.title: Install Cluster API components
steps.description:
This step installs the Cluster API components in the specified namespace.

```bash

helm install cluster-api cluster-api/cluster-api --namespace kube-system --version v1.12.5

```

> This installs the Cluster API components in the kube-system namespace.
commands: helm install cluster-api cluster-api/cluster-api --namespace kube-system --version v1.12.5

steps.title: Create a Cluster API custom resource
steps.description:
This step creates a Cluster API custom resource to manage your Kubernetes cluster.

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: cluster.x-k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Cluster
metadata:
  name: my-cluster
  namespace: default
spec:
  infrastructureRef:
    apiVersion: infrastructure.cluster.x-k8s.io/v1beta1
    kind: MyInfrastructureProvider
    name: my-infrastructure
EOF

```

> Replace 'MyInfrastructureProvider' with your actual infrastructure provider.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: cluster.x-k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Cluster
metadata:
  name: my-cluster
  namespace: default
spec:
  infrastructureRef:
    apiVersion: infrastructure.cluster.x-k8s.io/v1beta1
    kind: MyInfrastructureProvider
    name: my-infrastructure
EOF

steps.title: Verify Cluster API installation
steps.description:
This step checks if the Cluster API components are running correctly.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n kube-system

```

> You should see the Cluster API components running.

uninstall.title: Uninstall Cluster API
uninstall.description:
This step removes the Cluster API components and CRDs. Be aware that this will result in data loss for any clusters managed by Cluster API.

```bash

helm uninstall cluster-api --namespace kube-system

kubectl delete -f https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/cluster-api/releases/download/v1.12.5/cluster-api.crds.yaml

```

upgrade.title: Upgrade Cluster API
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you back up your cluster resources. This step will upgrade the Cluster API components to the latest version.

```bash

kubectl get all -o yaml > backup.yaml

helm upgrade cluster-api cluster-api/cluster-api --namespace kube-system --version v1.12.5

kubectl get pods -n kube-system

```

> Backup current resources.
> This upgrades the Cluster API components.
> Verify that the pods are running after the upgrade.
commands: helm upgrade cluster-api cluster-api/cluster-api --namespace kube-system --version v1.12.5

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'no matches for kind "Cluster" in version "cluster.x-k8s.io/v1beta1"'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the Cluster CRD has not been installed correctly.

**Fix:**
Reapply the CRDs using the command: kubectl apply -f https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/cluster-api/releases/download/v1.12.5/cluster-api.crds.yaml

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'failed to create cluster: infrastructure provider not found'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the specified infrastructure provider is not installed or configured.

**Fix:**
Ensure that the correct infrastructure provider is installed and referenced in your Cluster custom resource.

troubleshooting.title: Pods in kube-system are in 'CrashLoopBackOff' state
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This may occur due to misconfiguration or missing dependencies.

**Fix:**
Check the logs of the failing pods using: kubectl logs <pod-name> -n kube-system

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'unable to connect to the server: x509: certificate signed by unknown authority'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates a problem with the kubeconfig or the API server certificate.

**Fix:**
Ensure your kubeconfig is correctly set up and points to the right cluster context.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for new infrastructure providers and improved the handling of cluster upgrades.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated the old API version 'v1beta1' for certain resources.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from v1.11.x, ensure to update your custom resources to the new API version.

resolution.summary: You should see the output of 'kubectl get clusters' showing your cluster with the status 'Provisioned'.
codeSnippets: helm install cluster-api cluster-api/cluster-api --namespace kube-system --version v1.12.5

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: cluster.x-k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Cluster
metadata:
  name: my-cluster
  namespace: default
spec:
  infrastructureRef:
    apiVersion: infrastructure.cluster.x-k8s.io/v1beta1
    kind: MyInfrastructureProvider
    name: my-infrastructure
EOF

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.26 or higher and have kubectl and helm installed.
